text.title: Scalability Patterns and Async Processing
text.summary: Horizontal vs vertical scaling trade-offs, data partitioning strategies, Kubernetes HPA, connection pool sizing, and async processing patterns for offloading heavy work from the request path.

list.item: Horizontal (scale out): add instances. Limit: near-infinite. Downtime: zero. Complexity: higher (stateless design, service discovery required). Cost: linear. Best for: web APIs, stateless services.

Vertical (scale up): increase CPU/RAM per instance. Limit: hardware ceiling. Downtime: usually required. Complexity: lower. Cost: exponential at high tiers. Best for: databases, legacy apps, stateful services.

rule.statement: Horizontal scaling requires stateless application design: sessions externalized to Redis, no in-process shared mutable state, no local file writes for shared data.
rule.rationale: Horizontal scaling breaks sticky assumptions — any instance can receive any request. In-process state becomes a consistency bug, not just a performance issue.

list.item: Horizontal sharding: route by key range or hash. Example: users A-M → shard 1, users N-Z → shard 2. Pro: write throughput scales linearly. Con: cross-shard queries are expensive.

Vertical partitioning: split by domain. Example: user profiles → Users DB, orders → Orders DB, analytics → ClickHouse. Pro: each store optimized for its access pattern. Con: cross-domain joins require application-layer merging.
HPA scales pods based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics (via KEDA for queue depth, RPS, etc.).
K8s 1.33+: configurable tolerance for scale-up and scale-down decisions — prevents oscillation on noisy metrics.
Combine HPA with Cluster Autoscaler for infrastructure scaling; HPA handles pod count, Cluster Autoscaler handles node count.
Cost savings: 50-70% with well-tuned autoscaling vs always-on over-provisioned cluster.
Cold-start latency is the key risk: if pod startup takes 30s, a spike can exhaust capacity before scale-out completes. Pre-scale before known traffic events.

rule.statement: Never set connection pool size equal to the number of concurrent request handlers (threads or async tasks). The pool interacts with the DB thread count, not the app concurrency.
rule.rationale: An oversized pool exhausts DB connection slots; an undersized pool queues requests behind pool acquisition. Use the empirical formula: (core_count × 2) + spindles.

list.item: For SSDs (spindles ≈ 1): pool_size = core_count × 2. Typical result: 10–50 connections per app instance.
PgBouncer (PostgreSQL): transaction-mode pooling; one DB connection serves N app clients in round-robin. Use when app connection count exceeds DB max_connections.
ProxySQL (MySQL): connection multiplexing with query routing rules for read/write splitting.
SQLAlchemy (Python): pool_size (persistent) + max_overflow (burst headroom). Set pool_timeout to fail fast rather than queue indefinitely.

rule.statement: Move any operation taking > 200ms off the synchronous request path. Return 202 Accepted immediately, enqueue the work, notify via webhook or polling endpoint.
rule.rationale: Long-synchronous paths block server threads, inflate p99 latency, and cause cascading timeouts at the caller. Async decoupling is the fix, not increasing thread pool size.

list.item: Background jobs pattern: HTTP → API → 202 Accepted → Queue → Worker → heavy processing → notify. Use Celery (Python), BullMQ (Node), Sidekiq (Ruby).
Batch writes: replace N individual DB inserts in a loop with bulk_insert / COPY for N rows. 10-100x throughput improvement for large datasets.

Message queue selection: Kafka for event replay + ordered streams; RabbitMQ for complex routing + RPC; Redis Streams for simple queues + low latency (<1ms); SQS/Service Bus for serverless cloud-native.
Streaming processing (Flink, Spark Streaming): for real-time aggregations over high-volume event streams. Use only when the processing graph exceeds what a simple consumer can handle.

example.code:
# Bad: N database calls in a loop for item in items: db.insert(item) # 1000 items = 1000 round-trips
# Good: single batch call db.bulk_insert(items) # 1000 items = 1 round-trip
